title: Paradise Lost - Book II
content:
آ آ آ آ High on a Throne of Royal State, which far
آ آ آ آ Outshon the wealth of Ormus and of Ind,
آ آ آ آ Or where the gorgeous East with richest hand
آ آ آ آ Showrs on her Kings Barbaric Pearl and Gold,
آ آ آ آ Satan exalted sat, by merit raisï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To that bad eminence; and from despair
آ آ آ آ Thus high uplifted beyond hope, aspires
آ آ آ آ Beyond thus high, insatiate to pursue
آ آ آ آ Vain Warr with Heavï؟½n, and by success untaught
آ آ آ آ His proud imaginations thus displaid.
آ آ آ آ Powers and Dominions, Deities of Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ For since no deep within her gulf can hold
آ آ آ آ Immortal vigor, though opprest and fallï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ I give not Heavï؟½n for lost. From this descent
آ آ آ آ Celestial vertues rising, will appear
آ آ آ آ More glorious and more dread then from no fall,
آ آ آ آ And trust themselves to fear no second fate:
آ آ آ آ Mee though just right, and the fixt Laws of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Did first create your Leader, next, free choice,
آ آ آ آ With what besides, in Counsel or in Fight,
آ آ آ آ Hath bin achievd of merit, yet this loss
آ آ آ آ Thus farr at least recoverï؟½d, hath much more
آ آ آ آ Establisht in a safe unenvied Throne
آ آ آ آ Yeilded with full consent. The happier state
آ آ آ آ In Heavï؟½n, which follows dignity, might draw
آ آ آ آ Envy from each inferior; but who here
آ آ آ آ Will envy whom the highest place exposes
آ آ آ آ Formost to stand against the Thunderers aim
آ آ آ آ Your bulwark, and condemns to greatest share
آ آ آ آ Of endless pain? where there is then no good
آ آ آ آ For which to strive, no strife can grow up there
آ آ آ آ From Faction; for none sure will claim in hell
آ آ آ آ Precedence, none, whose portion is so small
آ آ آ آ Of present pain, that with ambitious mind
آ آ آ آ Will covet more. With this advantage then
آ آ آ آ To union, and firm Faith, and firm accord,
آ آ آ آ More then can be in Heavï؟½n, we now return
آ آ آ آ To claim our just inheritance of old,
آ آ آ آ Surer to prosper then prosperity
آ آ آ آ Could have assurï؟½d us; and by what best way,
آ آ آ آ Whether of open Warr or covert guile,
آ آ آ آ We now debate; who can advise, may speak.
آ آ آ آ He ceasï؟½d, and next him Moloc, Scepterï؟½d King
آ آ آ آ Stood up, the strongest and the fiercest Spirit
آ آ آ آ That fought in Heavï؟½n; now fiercer by despair:
آ آ آ آ His trust was with thï؟½ Eternal to be deemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Equal in strength, and rather then be less
آ آ آ آ Carï؟½d not to be at all; with that care lost
آ آ آ آ Went all his fear: of God, or Hell, or worse
آ آ آ آ He reckd not, and these words thereafter spake.
آ آ آ آ My sentence is for open Warr: Of Wiles,
آ آ آ آ More unexpert, I boast not: them let those
آ آ آ آ Contrive who need, or when they need, not now.
آ آ آ آ For while they sit contriving, shall the rest,
آ آ آ آ Millions that stand in Arms, and longing wait
آ آ آ آ The Signal to ascend, sit lingring here
آ آ آ آ Heavï؟½ns fugitives, and for thir dwelling place
آ آ آ آ Accept this dark opprobrious Den of shame,
آ آ آ آ The Prison of his Tyranny who Reigns
آ آ آ آ By our delay? no, let us rather choose
آ آ آ آ Armï؟½d with Hell flames and fury all at once
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½re Heavï؟½ns high Towrs to force resistless way,
آ آ آ آ Turning our Tortures into horrid Arms
آ آ آ آ Against the Torturer; when to meet the noise
آ آ آ آ Of his Almighty Engin he shall hear
آ آ آ آ Infernal Thunder, and for Lightning see
آ آ آ آ Black fire and horror shot with equal rage
آ آ آ آ Among his Angels; and his Throne it self
آ آ آ آ Mixt with Tartarean Sulphur, and strange fire,
آ آ آ آ His own invented Torments. But perhaps
آ آ آ آ The way seems difficult and steep to scale
آ آ آ آ With upright wing against a higher foe.
آ آ آ آ Let such bethink them, if the sleepy drench
آ آ آ آ Of that forgetful Lake benumme not still,
آ آ آ آ That in our proper motion we ascend
آ آ آ آ Up to our native seat: descent and fall
آ آ آ آ To us is adverse. Who but felt of late
آ آ آ آ When the fierce Foe hung on our brokï؟½n Rear
آ آ آ آ Insulting, and pursuï؟½d us through the Deep,
آ آ آ آ With what compulsion and laborious flight
آ آ آ آ We sunk thus low? Thï؟½ ascent is easie then;
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ event is fearï؟½d; should we again provoke
آ آ آ آ Our stronger, some worse way his wrath may find
آ آ آ آ To our destruction: if there be in Hell
آ آ آ آ Fear to be worse destroyï؟½d: what can be worse
آ آ آ آ Then to dwell here, drivï؟½n out from bliss, condemnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In this abhorred deep to utter woe;
آ آ آ آ Where pain of unextinguishable fire
آ آ آ آ Must exercise us without hope of end
آ آ آ آ The Vassals of his anger, when the Scourge
آ آ آ آ Inexorably, and the torturing hour
آ آ آ آ Calls us to Penance? More destroyï؟½d then thus
آ آ آ آ We should be quite abolisht and expire.
آ آ آ آ What fear we then? what doubt we to incense
آ آ آ آ His utmost ire? which to the highth enragï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Will either quite consume us, and reduce
آ آ آ آ To nothing this essential, happier farr
آ آ آ آ Then miserable to have eternal being:
آ آ آ آ Or if our substance be indeed Divine,
آ آ آ آ And cannot cease to be, we are at worst
آ آ آ آ On this side nothing; and by proof we feel
آ آ آ آ Our power sufficient to disturb his Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ And with perpetual inrodes to Allarme,
آ آ آ آ Though inaccessible, his fatal Throne:
آ آ آ آ Which if not Victory is yet Revenge.
آ آ آ آ He ended frowning, and his look denouncï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Desperate revenge, and Battel dangerous
آ آ آ آ To less then Gods. On thï؟½ other side up rose
آ آ آ آ Belial, in act more graceful and humane;
آ آ آ آ A fairer person lost not Heavï؟½n; he seemd
آ آ آ آ For dignity composï؟½d and high exploit:
آ آ آ آ But all was false and hollow; though his Tongue
آ آ آ آ Dropt Manna, and could make the worse appear
آ آ آ آ The better reason, to perplex and dash
آ آ آ آ Maturest Counsels: for his thoughts were low;
آ آ آ آ To vice industrious, but to Nobler deeds
آ آ آ آ Timorous and slothful: yet he pleasï؟½d the ear,
آ آ آ آ And with perswasive accent thus began.
آ آ آ آ I should be much for open Warr, O Peers,
آ آ آ آ As not behind in hate; if what was urgï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Main reason to perswade immediate Warr,
آ آ آ آ Did not disswade me most, and seem to cast
آ آ آ آ Ominous conjecture on the whole success:
آ آ آ آ When he who most excels in fact of Arms,
آ آ آ آ In what he counsels and in what excels
آ آ آ آ Mistrustful, grounds his courage on despair
آ آ آ آ And utter dissolution, as the scope
آ آ آ آ Of all his aim, after some dire revenge.
آ آ آ آ First, what Revenge? the Towrs of Heavï؟½n are fillï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With Armed watch, that render all access
آ آ آ آ Impregnable; oft on the bordering Deep
آ آ آ آ Encamp thir Legions, or with obscure wing
آ آ آ آ Scout farr and wide into the Realm of night,
آ آ آ آ Scorning surprize. Or could we break our way
آ آ آ آ By force, and at our heels all Hell should rise
آ آ آ آ With blackest Insurrection, to confound
آ آ آ آ Heavï؟½ns purest Light, yet our great Enemy
آ آ آ آ All incorruptible would on his Throne
آ آ آ آ Sit unpolluted, and thï؟½ Ethereal mould
آ آ آ آ Incapable of stain would soon expel
آ آ آ آ Her mischief, and purge off the baser fire
آ آ آ آ Victorious. Thus repulsï؟½d, our final hope
آ آ آ آ Is flat despair: we must exasperate
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Almighty Victor to spend all his rage,
آ آ آ آ And that must end us, that must be our cure,
آ آ آ آ To be no more; sad cure; for who would loose,
آ آ آ آ Though full of pain, this intellectual being,
آ آ آ آ Those thoughts that wander through Eternity,
آ آ آ آ To perish rather, swallowd up and lost
آ آ آ آ In the wide womb of uncreated night,
آ آ آ آ Devoid of sense and motion? and who knows,
آ آ آ آ Let this be good, whether our angry Foe
آ آ آ آ Can give it, or will ever? how he can
آ آ آ آ Is doubtful; that he never will is sure.
آ آ آ آ Will he, so wise, let loose at once his ire,
آ آ آ آ Belike through impotence, or unaware,
آ آ آ آ To give his Enemies thir wish, and end
آ آ آ آ Them in his anger, whom his anger saves
آ آ آ آ To punish endless? wherefore cease we then?
آ آ آ آ Say they who counsel Warr, we are decreed,
آ آ آ آ Reservï؟½d and destinï؟½d to Eternal woe;
آ آ آ آ Whatever doing, what can we suffer more,
آ آ آ آ What can we suffer worse? is this then worst,
آ آ آ آ Thus sitting, thus consulting, thus in Arms?
آ آ آ آ What when we fled amain, pursuï؟½d and strook
آ آ آ آ With Heavï؟½ns afflicting Thunder, and besought
آ آ آ آ The Deep to shelter us? this Hell then seemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ A refuge from those wounds: or when we lay
آ آ آ آ Chainï؟½d on the burning Lake? that sure was worse.
آ آ آ آ What if the breath that kindlï؟½d those grim fires
آ آ آ آ Awakï؟½d should blow them into sevenfold rage
آ آ آ آ And plunge us in the Flames? or from above
آ آ آ آ Should intermitted vengeance arm again
آ آ آ آ His red right hand to plague us? what if all
آ آ آ آ Her stores were opï؟½nï؟½d, and this Firmament
آ آ آ آ Of Hell should spout her Cataracts of Fire,
آ آ آ آ Impendent horrors, threatning hideous fall
آ آ آ آ One day upon our heads; while we perhaps
آ آ آ آ Designing or exhorting glorious Warr,
آ آ آ آ Caught in a fierie Tempest shall be hurlï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Each on his rock transfixt, the sport and prey
آ آ آ آ Of racking whirlwinds, or for ever sunk
آ آ آ آ Under yon boyling Ocean, wrapt in Chains;
آ آ آ آ There to converse with everlasting groans,
آ آ آ آ Unrespited, unpitied, unrepreevd,
آ آ آ آ Ages of hopeless end; this would be worse.
آ آ آ آ Warr therefore, open or concealï؟½d, alike
آ آ آ آ My voice disswades; for what can force or guile
آ آ آ آ With him, or who deceive his mind, whose eye
آ آ آ آ Views all things at one view? he from heavï؟½ns highth
آ آ آ آ All these our motions vain, sees and derides;
آ آ آ آ Not more Almighty to resist our might
آ آ آ آ Then wise to frustrate all our plots and wiles.
آ آ آ آ Shall we then live thus vile, the race of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Thus tramplï؟½d, thus expellï؟½d to suffer here
آ آ آ آ Chains and these Torments? better these then worse
آ آ آ آ By my advice; since fate inevitable
آ آ آ آ Subdues us, and Omnipotent Decree,
آ آ آ آ The Victors will. To suffer, as to doe,
آ آ آ آ Our strength is equal, nor the Law unjust
آ آ آ آ That so ordains: this was at first resolvï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ If we were wise, against so great a foe
آ آ آ آ Contending, and so doubtful what might fall.
آ آ آ آ I laugh, when those who at the Spear are bold
آ آ آ آ And ventï؟½rous, if that fail them, shrink and fear
آ آ آ آ What yet they know must follow, to endure
آ آ آ آ Exile, or ignominy, or bonds, or pain,
آ آ آ آ The sentence of thir Conquerour: This is now
آ آ آ آ Our doom; which if we can sustain and bear,
آ آ آ آ Our Supream Foe in time may much remit
آ آ آ آ His anger, and perhaps thus farr removï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Not mind us not offending, satisfiï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With what is punishï؟½t; whence these raging fires
آ آ آ آ Will slackï؟½n, if his breath stir not thir flames.
آ آ آ آ Our purer essence then will overcome
آ آ آ آ Thir noxious vapour, or enurï؟½d not feel,
آ آ آ آ Or changï؟½d at length, and to the place conformd
آ آ آ آ In temper and in nature, will receive
آ آ آ آ Familiar the fierce heat, and void of pain;
آ آ آ آ This horror will grow milde, this darkness light,
آ آ آ آ Besides what hope the never-ending flight
آ آ آ آ Of future days may bring, what chance, what change
آ آ آ آ Worth waiting, since our present lot appeers
آ آ آ آ For happy though but ill, for ill not worst,
آ آ آ آ If we procure not to our selves more woe.
آ آ آ آ Thus Belial with words cloathï؟½d in reasons garb
آ آ آ آ Counselï؟½d ignoble ease, and peaceful sloath,
آ آ آ آ Not peace: and after him thus Mammon spake.
آ آ آ آ Either to disinthrone the King of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ We warr, if warr be best, or to regain
آ آ آ آ Our own right lost: him to unthrone we then
آ آ آ آ May hope, when everlasting Fate shall yeild
آ آ آ آ To fickle Chance, and Chaos judge the strife:
آ آ آ آ The former vain to hope argues as vain
آ آ آ آ The latter: for what place can be for us
آ آ آ آ Within Heavï؟½ns bound, unless Heavï؟½ns Lord supream
آ آ آ آ We overpower? Suppose he should relent
آ آ آ آ And publish Grace to all, on promise made
آ آ آ آ Of new Subjection; with what eyes could we
آ آ آ آ Stand in his presence humble, and receive
آ آ آ آ Strict Laws imposï؟½d, to celebrate his Throne
آ آ آ آ With warblï؟½d Hymns, and to his Godhead sing
آ آ آ آ Forcï؟½t Halleluiahï؟½s; while he Lordly sits
آ آ آ آ Our envied Sovran, and his Altar breathes
آ آ آ آ Ambrosial Odours and Ambrosial Flowers,
آ آ آ آ Our servile offerings. This must be our task
آ آ آ آ In Heavï؟½n, this our delight; how wearisom
آ آ آ آ Eternity so spent in worship paid
آ آ آ آ To whom we hate. Let us not then pursue
آ آ آ آ By force impossible, by leave obtainï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Unacceptable, though in Heavï؟½n, our state
آ آ آ آ Of splendid vassalage, but rather seek
آ آ آ آ Our own good from our selves, and from our own
آ آ آ آ Live to our selves, though in this vast recess,
آ آ آ آ Free, and to none accountable, preferring
آ آ آ آ Hard liberty before the easie yoke
آ آ آ آ Of servile Pomp. Our greatness will appear
آ آ آ آ Then most conspicuous, when great things of small,
آ آ آ آ Useful of hurtful, prosperous of adverse
آ آ آ آ We can create, and in what place so eï؟½re
آ آ آ آ Thrive under evil, and work ease out of pain
آ آ آ آ Through labour and endurance. This deep world
آ آ آ آ Of darkness do we dread? How oft amidst
آ آ آ آ Thick clouds and dark doth Heavï؟½ns all-ruling Sire
آ آ آ آ Choose to reside, his Glory unobscurï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And with the Majesty of darkness round
آ آ آ آ Covers his Throne; from whence deep thunders roar
آ آ آ آ Mustï؟½ring thir rage, and Heavï؟½n resembles Hell?
آ آ آ آ As he our Darkness, cannot we his Light
آ آ آ آ Imitate when we please? This Desart soile
آ آ آ آ Wants not her hidden lustre, Gemms and Gold;
آ آ آ آ Nor want we skill or art, from whence to raise
آ آ آ آ Magnificence; and what can Heavï؟½n shew more?
آ آ آ آ Our torments also may in length of time
آ آ آ آ Become our Elements, these piercing Fires
آ آ آ آ As soft as now severe, our temper changï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Into their temper; which must needs remove
آ آ آ آ The sensible of pain. All things invite
آ آ آ آ To peaceful Counsels, and the settlï؟½d State
آ آ آ آ Of order, how in safety best we may
آ آ آ آ Compose our present evils, with regard
آ آ آ آ Of what we are and where, dismissing quite
آ آ آ آ All thoughts of Warr: ye have what I advise.
آ آ آ آ He scarce had finisht, when such murmur filld
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Assembly, as when hollow Rocks retain
آ آ آ آ The sound of blustring winds, which all night long
آ آ آ آ Had rousï؟½d the Sea, now with hoarse cadence lull
آ آ آ آ Sea-faring men orewatcht, whose Bark by chance
آ آ آ آ Or Pinnace anchors in a craggy Bay
آ آ آ آ After the Tempest: Such applause was heard
آ آ آ آ As Mammon ended, and his Sentence pleasï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Advising peace: for such another Field
آ آ آ آ They dreaded worse then Hell: so much the fear
آ آ آ آ Of Thunder and the Sword of Michael
آ آ آ آ Wrought still within them; and no less desire
آ آ آ آ To found this nether Empire, which might rise
آ آ آ آ By pollicy, and long process of time,
آ آ آ آ In emulation opposite to Heavï؟½n.
آ آ آ آ Which when Beelzebub perceivï؟½d, then whom,
آ آ آ آ Satan except, none higher sat, with grave
آ آ آ آ Aspect he rose, and in his rising seemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ A Pillar of State; deep on his Front engraven
آ آ آ آ Deliberation sat and public care;
آ آ آ آ And Princely counsel in his face yet shon,
آ آ آ آ Majestick though in ruin: sage he stood
آ آ آ آ With Atlantean shoulders fit to bear
آ آ آ آ The weight of mightiest Monarchies; his look
آ آ آ آ Drew audience and attention still as Night
آ آ آ آ Or Summers Noon-tide air, while thus he spake.
آ آ آ آ Thrones and imperial Powers, off-spring of heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ Ethereal Vertues; or these Titles now
آ آ آ آ Must we renounce, and changing stile be callï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Princes of Hell? for so the popular vote
آ آ آ آ Inclines, here to continue, and build up here
آ آ آ آ A growing Empire; doubtless; while we dream,
